macro_segments.title: The Law of Bar Metzra (Adjoining Plot) in Inheritance Divisions

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Case: Two Brothers Dividing an Inherited Estate with Adjacent Plots
Rasha's View: Bar Metzra Applies to Inheritance Division
Rav Yosef's Rejection: Bar Metzra Does Not Apply to Inheritance
Rashi and Tosafot's Explanations of the Dispute
Halakhic Ruling: We Follow Rav Yosef

macro_segments.title: Division of Two Plots Adjacent to Two Different Irrigation Canals

micro_segments.title: Setting the Scenario: Two Plots with a Canal at Each End
Raba's Initial Proposal: Divide North-South to Preserve Continuity
Rav Yosef's Challenge: Risk Management Through Dual Canal Access
Halakhic Ruling: Follow Rav Yosef; Division Need Not Be North-South

macro_segments.title: Division of Two Plots Sharing a Single Irrigation Canal

micro_segments.title: The Standard Case: Dividing Down the Center of a Single Canal
Abaye's Counter-Objection: Desire for More Sharecroppers on One's Field
Gemara's Response: Sharecropper Arguments Are Not Valid Grounds
Comparison to the Earlier Case: Why Risk Management Differs from Sharecropper Claims

macro_segments.title: Division of Land with Perpendicular Water Sources (Canal and River)

micro_segments.title: Rashi's Complex Case: Perpendicular Water Sources Requiring Diagonal Division
Tosafot's Simpler Case: Opposite Corners with Diagonal Split
The Requirement: Each Party Must Receive a Complete, Usable Unit

macro_segments.title: Gud O Agud (Buy-Out or Be Bought Out) When Division Is Impossible

micro_segments.title: The Core Question: What Happens When Inheritance Cannot Be Divided Equally?
Rav Yehuda's Position: Either Buy Me Out or I Buy You Out
Rav Nachman's Objection: Cannot Force Someone Out of Partnership
Rationale: Financial Hardship and Livelihood Concerns
Tosafot's Point: Price Must Be Objectively Set, Not Arbitrary

macro_segments.title: Rava's Challenge to Rav Nachman Using the Bechor and Pashut (Firstborn and Non-Firstborn) Case

micro_segments.title: The Problem: Firstborn Gets Double Share; Inheritance Includes Non-Divisible Items
Why This Proves Gud O Agud Must Exist: Alternative Is Untenable
Rav Nachman's Response: Accept Inconvenience of Alternating Days

macro_segments.title: The Half-Slave, Half-Free Man (Eved Kna'ani Mishnah from Gittin)

micro_segments.title: Background: Two Partners with Different Interests in Ownership
Beis Hillel's Solution: Alternating Days of Work and Profit
Beis Shammai's Objection: This Does Not Help the Slave's Condition
The Marriage Problem: Slave Cannot Marry Slave or Free Woman
God's Creation Purpose: World Made for Fruitfulness and Multiplication
Solution: Force Master to Free the Slave and Owe Him Compensation
Beis Hillel Reverts to Beis Shammai's Position

macro_segments.title: Why Gud O Agud Cannot Apply to the Half-Slave Case: Asymmetry of Options
macro_segments.display_title: Why Gud o Agud Fails Here

micro_segments.title: The Challenge: If Gud O Agud Applies, Why Not Just Buy and Sell?
The Key Distinction: Agud (I Buy You Out) Works; Gud (You Buy Me Out) Does Not
Why Gud Fails: A Jew Cannot Be Sold as Eved Kna'ani
Tosafot's Extended Discussion on Piryah v'Rivyah and Obligations of Slaves

macro_segments.title: The Rich Brother and Poor Brother Dividing a Bathhouse and Olive Press

micro_segments.title: Case One: Bathhouse and Olive Press Used as Commercial Revenue
Case Two: Private Use; Rich Brother's Ability to Use vs. Poor Brother's Inability
The Rich Brother's Apparent Rights: Use with His Own Slaves or Olives
Gemara's Analysis: Gud Exists (I Buy You Out) But Not Agud (You Buy Me Out)
Reason: Poor Person Cannot Genuinely Offer to Buy Rich Person Out

macro_segments.title: Debate of Tannaim on Unequal Division and Tosafot's Interpretation of the Braita

micro_segments.title: Tannaim Dispute: Can One Accept Less to Exit Partnership Without Payment?
Tanna Kamma: Yes, Acceptance of Less Land Is Valid Exit Method
Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel: No, Cannot Accept Gift or Unequal Terms
Gemara's Reconstruction: Missing Text Includes Gud O Agud as Alternative
Alternative Reading: Text Is Complete; Dispute Is About Unequal Land Division
Poor Person's Refusal: 'I Don't Have Money' and 'I Don't Want Gifts'

macro_segments.title: The Scrolls (Kisvei Kodesh) Case: Can You Have Torah, Nevi'im, and Kesuvim in One Scroll?

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's Teaching: One Scroll Cannot Be Divided; Two Scrolls Can Be
Abaye's Proof: Two Different Scrolls (Esther and Kohelet) Suggests Payment Option

macro_segments.title: Ameymar Rules that Gud O Agud Applies; Rav Ashi Challenges with Rav Nachman's View

micro_segments.title: Ameymar's Ruling: We Do Accept the Principle of Gud O Agud
Rav Ashi's Challenge: What About Rav Nachman's Opposing View?
Ameymar's Response: I Don't Hold of Rav Nachman's Position

macro_segments.title: Counter-Example: Rabba Bar Chinana and Rav Dimi Bar Chinana Dividing Two Maidservants

micro_segments.title: Setup: Two Brothers Inherit Maidservants with Different Skills
Rava's Ruling: No Gud O Agud; Cannot Force Purchase of One Servant
Why Rava's Case Differs: Both Brothers Arguably Want Both Servants
Key Principle: Gud O Agud for Whole; Not for Forcing Partial Split with Payment

macro_segments.title: The Scrolls (Kisvei Kodesh) Case: Can You Have Torah, Nevi'im, and Kesuvim in One Scroll? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Question: Does Gud O Agud Allow One Person to Take Both Scrolls?
Response: Shmuel Only Means Both Agree to Divide; No Forced Division

macro_segments.title: Tannaim Debate on Attaching All of Tanakh in One Scroll

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir: Permitted to Have All of Torah, Nevi'im, and Kesuvim Together
Rabbi Yehuda: Each Must Be Separate for Proper Identity and Honor
Tosafot's Explanation: Concern About Confusion of Textual Authority
Additional Requirements: Four Blank Lines Between Major Sections

macro_segments.title: Practical Requirements for Attaching Multiple Scrolls into One Combined Scroll

micro_segments.title: Top Requirement: Blank Space to Roll Over the Upper Pole
Bottom Requirement: Blank Space Adequate for Entire Circumference When Rolled
Braita: Procedures for Creating a Combined Tanakh Scroll
Separation Rule: End Each Book at Bottom of Column; Start New Book at Top
Purpose of Separation: Allow Future Division If Necessary

topical_tags.term: Gud O Agud (Buy Out or Be Bought Out)
Rabbi Meir vs. Rabbi Yehuda (Scroll Division)
